subject: History
chapter: Kings and Chronicles: The Mughal Courts

concepts.title: Establishment of Mughal Empire
concepts.description: The Mughal Empire was established by Babur in 1526 after the Battle of Panipat, marking the beginning of a new era in Indian history.

concepts.title: Akbar's Reforms
concepts.description: Akbar introduced several significant reforms, including the establishment of the Ibadat Khana, the promotion of Sufism, and the creation of a centralized administrative system.

concepts.title: Jahangir's Regime
concepts.description: Jahangir's regime is known for its tolerance towards different cultures and religions, as evident from his treatment of the Sikh Guru Arjan Dev and the Sufi saint Mulla Shah.

concepts.title: Mughal Administrative Structure
concepts.description: The Mughal administrative structure consisted of the imperial court, the provincial administration, and the local governance system, which was based on the concept of 'diwani' and 'faujdar'.

concepts.title: Shah Jahan's Architectural Legacy
concepts.description: Shah Jahan is famous for his architectural legacy, including the construction of the Taj Mahal, which is a symbol of love and beauty in Indian history.

concepts.title: Mughal Emperors' Succession
concepts.description: To remember the Mughal emperors' succession, use the mnemonic 'B-A-J-S-A-U', which stands for Babur, Akbar, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, Aurangzeb, and Udaipur (but Udaipur is not part of the Mughal dynasty). Instead, correct is Aurangzeb.

concepts.title: Decline of Mughal Empire
concepts.description: The decline of the Mughal Empire can be attributed to the internal conflicts, external invasions, and the lack of a strong and capable successor to Aurangzeb.

concepts.title: Mughal Economic System
concepts.description: Students often mistake the Mughal economic system as being based solely on agriculture, when in fact, it was a mixed economy that included agriculture, trade, and industry.
